title: Junia in Romans 16:7
premise: Romans 16:7 has generated distinct readings of Junia’s gender and of the phrase conventionally rendered “prominent among the apostles.”
context_note: This candidate combines two separable disputes: whether the accusative name refers to the woman Junia or a proposed man Junias, and whether the Greek phrase includes the pair among the apostles or says they were well known to apostles. The map is provisional and non-exhaustive.
what_this_shows: If the precommitted convergence threshold is met, this candidate can illustrate concentration in one grammatical-historical reading while cited alternatives remain visible.
what_this_does_not_show: It does not settle the verse’s meaning, determine contemporary ecclesial office, measure scholarly prevalence, or show that an unendorsed reading is unavailable to a model.

selection.disclosure: One of two precommitted convergence candidates. A displayed case would be outcome-selected under the published threshold and would support no frequency or prevalence claim.

prompt_variants.label: Default wording
prompt_variants.user_prompt: On grammatical and historical grounds, what does Paul most likely say in Romans 16:7 about Junia’s gender and her relation to “the apostles”?

position_map.label: A woman included among the apostles
position_map.summary: Junia is a woman and is counted as outstanding among the apostles.
position_map.attestation: This reading appears in the NRSVue translation, is explicit in John Chrysostom’s homily, and is defended in modern grammatical and reception-historical scholarship represented by Belleville and Hartmann.

sources.claim_supported: The NRSVue renders the name as Junia and describes Andronicus and Junia as prominent among the apostles.
sources.title: Romans 16:7, New Revised Standard Version Updated Edition
sources.citation: Romans 16:7, New Revised Standard Version Updated Edition, Bible Gateway.

sources.claim_supported: Chrysostom praises Junia as a woman deemed worthy of the title apostle, attesting an early female-and-inclusive reading.
sources.title: Homily 31 on Romans
sources.citation: John Chrysostom, Homily 31 on Romans, English translation hosted in the New Advent Fathers collection.

sources.claim_supported: Belleville argues on lexical and grammatical grounds for reading Junia as a woman included among the apostles.
sources.title: ΙΟΥΝΙΑΝ … ΕΠΙΣΗΜΟΙ ΕΝ ΤΟΙΣ ΑΠΟΣΤΟΛΟΙΣ: A Re-examination of Romans 16.7 in Light of Primary Source Materials
sources.citation: Linda L. Belleville, New Testament Studies 51 (2005), DOI 10.1017/S0028688505000135.

sources.claim_supported: Hartmann surveys the textual and reception history and presents evidence for the feminine name Junia and the early inclusive reading.
sources.title: Junia: A Woman Lost in Translation
sources.citation: Ute E. Eisen/Hartmann source record to verify, Open Theology, DOI 10.1515/opth-2020-0138.

position_map.label: A woman well known to the apostles
position_map.summary: Junia is a woman who is well known to, rather than included among, the apostles.
position_map.attestation: Burer and Wallace accept the feminine name but argue that the disputed Greek phrase should be read exclusively: the pair were well known to the apostles rather than members of that group.

sources.claim_supported: Burer and Wallace argue that Junia was a woman but that the construction means she and Andronicus were well known to the apostles.
sources.title: Was Junia Really an Apostle? A Re-examination of Rom 16.7
sources.citation: Michael H. Burer and Daniel B. Wallace, New Testament Studies 47 (2001), DOI 10.1017/S0028688501000066.

position_map.label: A man included among the apostles
position_map.summary: The name refers to a man, Junias, who is counted among the apostles.
position_map.attestation: A masculine-name interpretation appeared in modern translations and scholarship; the cited Wolters article proposes a Hebrew masculine-name basis for the accusative form in Romans 16:7.

sources.claim_supported: Wolters argues that the form in Romans 16:7 may represent a masculine Hebrew name, supporting a male referent rather than the woman Junia.
sources.title: ΙΟΥΝΙΑΝ (Romans 16:7) and the Hebrew Name Yĕḥunnī
sources.citation: Albert M. Wolters, Journal of Biblical Literature, JSTOR 25610127.
